abstract: The integration of large language models (LLMs) with embodied agents has improved high-level reasoning capabilities; however, a critical gap remains between semantic understanding and physical execution. While vision-language-action (VLA) and vision-language-navigation (VLN) systems enable robots to perform manipulation and navigation tasks from natural language instructions, they still struggle with long-horizon sequential and temporally structured tasks. Existing frameworks typically adopt modular pipelines for data collection, skill training, and policy deployment, resulting in high costs in experimental validation and policy optimization. To address these limitations, we propose ROSClaw, an agent framework for heterogeneous robots that integrates policy learning and task execution within a unified vision-language model (VLM) controller. The framework leverages e-URDF representations of heterogeneous robots as physical constraints to construct a sim-to-real topological mapping, enabling real-time access to the physical states of both simulated and real-world agents. We further incorporate a data collection and state accumulation mechanism that stores robot states, multimodal observations, and execution trajectories during real-world execution, enabling subsequent iterative policy optimization. During deployment, a unified agent maintains semantic continuity between reasoning and execution, and dynamically assigns task-specific control to different agents, thereby improving robustness in multi-policy execution. By establishing an autonomous closed-loop framework, ROSClaw minimizes the reliance on robot-specific development workflows. The framework supports hardware-level validation, automated generation of SDK-level control programs, and tool-based execution, enabling rapid cross-platform transfer and continual improvement of robotic skills. Ours project page: this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: M. Ahn, D. Dwibedi, C. Finn, M. G. Arenas, K. Gopalakrishnan, K. Hausman, B. Ichter, A. Irpan, N. Joshi, R. Julian et al., “Autort: Embodied foundation models for large scale orchestration of robotic agents,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2401.12963, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper is cited to highlight a key limitation in existing frameworks—the modularization of data collection, policy learning, and deployment into disjoint stages. ROSClaw directly addresses this limitation by proposing an integrated, closed-loop system, making AutoRT a critical piece of prior art that defines the problem space.
citations.title: Autort: Embodied foundation models for large scale orchestration of robotic agents

citations.fullCitation: Z. Mandi, S. Jain, and S. Song, “Roco: Dialectic multi-robot collaboration with large language models,” in 2024 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA). IEEE, 2024, pp. 286–299.
citations.justification: The ROSClaw paper discusses this work in its related works section as an example of a decentralized, debate-based structure for multi-robot collaboration. This makes it a highly relevant citation as it provides a direct point of comparison for different architectural approaches to multi-agent coordination using large language models.
citations.title: Roco: Dialectic multi-robot collaboration with large language models

citations.fullCitation: D. Driess, F. Xia, M. S. Sajjadi, C. Lynch, A. Chowdhery, B. Ichter, A. Wahid, J. Tompson, Q. Vuong, T. Yu et al., “Palm-e: An embodied multimodal language model,” arXiv preprint arXiv:2303.03378, 2023.
citations.justification: This work is cited as a foundational example of a Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model that provides end-to-end alignment between perception, language, and action. It represents a key technological pillar that enables the kind of semantic-to-physical execution that the ROSClaw framework is designed to manage and improve upon for long-horizon tasks.
citations.title: Palm-e: An embodied multimodal language model

citations.fullCitation: S. Hong, M. Zhuge, J. Chen, X. Zheng, Y. Cheng, J. Wang, C. Zhang, Z. Wang, S. K. S. Yau, Z. Lin et al., “Metagpt: Meta programming for a multi-agent collaborative framework,” in The twelfth international conference on learning representations, 2023.
citations.justification: This paper is referenced as a primary example of an early LLM-based multi-agent system that uses a hierarchical, top-down structure for task allocation. This provides crucial context for ROSClaw's contribution by contrasting these earlier, software-focused frameworks with its own embodied, semantic-physical approach for real-world robots.
citations.title: Metagpt: Meta programming for a multi-agent collaborative framework

intermediateReport:
## Detailed Report: ROSClaw: A Hierarchical Semantic-Physical Framework for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Collaboration

This report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "ROSClaw: A Hierarchical Semantic-Physical Framework for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Collaboration," outlining its contributions to the field of robotics and artificial intelligence.

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of this research are:

*   **Rongfeng Zhao**, **Xuanhao Zhang**, **Zhaochen Guo**, **Xiang Shao**: Affiliated with the Shanghai Research Institute for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Shanghai 201210, China; the National Key Laboratory of Autonomous Intelligent Unmanned Systems (Tongji University), Shanghai 201210, China; and the Frontiers Science Center for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Shanghai 201210, China. These authors contributed equally to the work.
*   **Zhongpan Zhu**: Affiliated with the Shanghai Research Institute for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Tongji University, Shanghai 201210, China; the National Key Laboratory of Autonomous Intelligent Unmanned Systems (Tongji University), Shanghai 201210, China; and the Frontiers Science Center for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Shanghai 201210, China. Zhongpan Zhu is a corresponding author.
*   **Bin He**: Affiliated with the College of Electronics and Information Engineering, Tongji University, Shanghai 201804, China; the Shanghai Research Institute for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Shanghai 201210, China; the National Key Laboratory of Autonomous Intelligent Unmanned Systems (Tongji University), Shanghai 201210, China; and the Frontiers Science Center for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Shanghai 201210, China. Bin He is a corresponding author and Senior Member, IEEE.
*   **Jie Chen**: Affiliated with the Shanghai Research Institute for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Shanghai 201210, China; the National Key Laboratory of Autonomous Intelligent Unmanned Systems (Tongji University), Shanghai 201210, China; and the Frontiers Science Center for Intelligent Autonomous Systems, Shanghai 201210, China. Jie Chen is a Fellow, IEEE.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The integration of large language models (LLMs) with embodied agents has advanced high-level reasoning capabilities in robotics, enabling robots to interpret natural language instructions for manipulation (VLA) and navigation (VLN) tasks. This development is progressively shifting the focus from single-agent task execution toward robust heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration, where diverse robots combine their complementary strengths (e.g., humanoid mobility with robotic arm precision).

Despite these advancements, a significant semantic-physical gap persists. Current frameworks often modularize data collection, skill training, and policy deployment into distinct, often disjointed stages. This modularity can introduce semantic and distributional inconsistencies, increasing experimental validation costs and hindering robustness, particularly for long-horizon sequential and temporally structured tasks. Many existing hierarchical embodied frameworks lack explicit physical constraints, which can lead to the generation of physically infeasible or unsafe task plans. Furthermore, direct interfaces between high-level semantic APIs and low-level hardware can introduce communication latency and limit high-frequency closed-loop control. The absence of a unified abstraction layer often necessitates extensive, hardware-specific tuning across heterogeneous platforms, limiting scalability and reusability. Moreover, while LLMs exhibit strong abstract reasoning, they frequently lack a grounded understanding of the physical constraints necessary for reliable action execution.

In the context of multi-agent systems, existing LLM-based approaches often rely on predefined communication structures and limit inter-agent collaboration to high-level task planning via API-level interactions, lacking tight integration with low-level physical control. Traditional multi-robot systems often struggle with generalization across dynamic environments and heterogeneous platforms due to their reliance on manually designed task decomposition and heuristic resource allocation. Even advanced vision-language-action (VLA) models and hierarchical planners are prone to error accumulation in long-horizon collaborative tasks, as supervision is typically applied at discrete subtask boundaries rather than providing continuous, process-level feedback during low-level execution. In multi-robot settings, large models are frequently employed as centralized schedulers, with execution delegated to fragmented pre-programmed APIs, resulting in weak coupling between high-level reasoning and physical control.

ROSClaw directly addresses these limitations by proposing a unified, hierarchical semantic-physical framework. It aims to bridge the semantic-physical gap by integrating policy learning and task execution within a single vision-language model (VLM) controller, incorporating explicit physical constraints through e-URDF representations, and enabling continuous learning through a closed-loop data collection and state accumulation mechanism. This approach aims to provide a more robust, adaptive, and efficient solution for heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration than existing methods.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary motivation for this work stems from the identified fundamental semantic-physical gap in current embodied intelligence systems. Specifically, the challenges include:
1.  **Semantic-Physical Mismatch:** Large models primarily operate in semantic and linguistic domains, lacking explicit representations of physical dynamics, control constraints, and temporal dependencies essential for reliable real-world execution.
2.  **Hardware Heterogeneity:** Fragmented SDKs and non-standardized interfaces across heterogeneous robotic platforms impede unified integration and efficient coordination.
3.  **Static Pipelines:** Existing frameworks are often designed as static, open-loop pipelines, preventing the conversion of physical interactions into reusable knowledge and limiting continuous adaptation and improvement.

To address these motivations, ROSClaw establishes several key objectives:

*   **Bridge High-Level Reasoning and Low-Level Control:** Develop a hierarchical framework that effectively connects the high-level cognitive reasoning of LLMs with high-frequency physical control.
*   **Unified Abstraction for Heterogeneous Robots:** Create a system that abstracts away the diversity of underlying hardware, enabling unified control and task assignment across different robotic platforms.
*   **Integrate Policy Learning and Task Execution:** Design a single agent loop that ensures contextual semantic consistency from planning to execution, facilitating iterative policy optimization and continual improvement.
*   **Enforce Physical Constraints and Safety:** Incorporate mechanisms, such as e-URDF representations and digital twin simulations, to validate physical feasibility of generated commands before execution, preventing unsafe or impossible actions.
*   **Enable Closed-Loop Learning:** Implement a data collection and state accumulation mechanism that continuously records robot states, multimodal observations, and execution trajectories. This data is then fed back to the cognitive layer, facilitating self-organizing learning and improving alignment between semantic reasoning and physical execution.
*   **Reduce Development Costs and Manual Intervention:** Minimize reliance on robot-specific development workflows, automate hardware validation, and synthesize SDK-level control programs, thereby reducing human effort in system adaptation.
*   **Promote Cross-Platform Generalization and Skill Transfer:** Utilize an Online Tool Pool to map abstract instructions to executable software calls, fostering a "train once, deploy everywhere" paradigm and enabling rapid transfer of robotic skills.

Ultimately, ROSClaw aims to transform embodied intelligence from isolated task execution into a resource-driven, closed-loop process centered on shared tools and reusable skills, supporting continuous system evolution.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

ROSClaw adopts a hierarchical semantic-physical agent framework designed for heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration in dynamic environments. The methodology is structured around a three-layer architecture and specific mechanisms for integration, validation, and learning:

**A. Three-Layer Semantic-Physical Architecture:**
The framework spans three distinct but interconnected layers:
1.  **Cognitive Layer:** Functions as the high-level decision engine, leveraging large language and vision-language models (LLMs/VLMs). This layer performs low-frequency task decomposition, long-horizon reasoning, and semantic understanding. It operates under a bidirectional interaction paradigm, generating structured semantic representations and task plans while receiving execution feedback, environmental states, and interaction outcomes from the physical world. This feedback loop facilitates the alignment of semantic reasoning with physical execution, fostering a grounded spatiotemporal understanding.
2.  **Coordination Automation Layer (OpenClaw System):** This layer acts as an intermediary, abstracting task logic and orchestrating automated tools.
    *   **Online Tool Pool:** A central component that aggregates various robot Software Development Kits (SDKs), Model Context Protocols (MCPs), and multi-system API interfaces. It serves as a "digital dictionary" that automatically translates abstract instructions from the cognitive layer into concrete software invocation procedures, enabling cross-platform generalization.
    *   **e-URDF-based Physical Interception and Scheduling Mechanism:** Before any actions are dispatched to the physical world, this mechanism ensures physical feasibility. It leverages a digital twin engine (specifically Isaac Lab) to perform forward dynamics simulation and collision detection using e-URDF representations of heterogeneous agents. This "physical firewall" filters out infeasible commands generated by the large models.
3.  **ROSClaw Physical World:** This layer interfaces directly with high-frequency robot operating systems (ROS) and platform-specific drivers, providing unified control over heterogeneous agents.
    *   **Local Resource Pool:** During execution, ROSClaw continuously collects real-time, high-frequency data, including robot physical states, multimodal observations (from physical-world sensors), and successful long-horizon execution trajectories. This data is standardized and consolidated into the Local Resource Pool, forming reusable skill representations. This mechanism supports continual policy refinement and cross-scenario generalization, and the accumulated data can be uploaded to a cloud-based model resource pool for large-scale model fine-tuning or training.

**B. Core Abstractions and Mechanisms:**

*   **Asynchronous Decoupling:** The framework decouples low-frequency semantic planning (cognitive layer) from high-frequency physical control (physical world) to enable stable interaction across different temporal scales.
*   **Resource-Driven Closed-Loop Process:** Embodied intelligence is modeled as a process centered on shared tools and reusable skills, where perception, reasoning, and execution are tightly coupled with data accumulation and continual learning.
*   **Unified Pipeline:** The methodology integrates e-URDF-based physical constraints with the data collection and state accumulation mechanisms to enable self-organizing learning. Physically grounded data is fed back into the cognitive layer to improve alignment between semantic reasoning and physical execution.
*   **Tool-Driven Execution and Program Synthesis:** The Online Tool Pool enables abstract instructions to be mapped to executable software calls, facilitating autonomous hardware validation and SDK-level program synthesis.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The ROSClaw framework was validated through real-world experiments involving heterogeneous multi-agent systems performing complex, multi-step tasks. The experiments demonstrated the framework's capabilities in collaborative operation, physical safeguarding, and data accumulation.

**A. Temporal Task Multi-Robot Collaborative Operation Validation:**
A collaborative task was conducted in a 60-square-meter smart home environment (kitchen and living room) involving three heterogeneous robots: a mobile robotic arm, a humanoid robot, and a fixed robotic arm. These robots were manufactured by different companies and used distinct drivers and development SDKs.
*   **Task Description:** The task involved a sequence of actions:
    1.  A mobile robotic arm approaches a doorway, uses its manipulator to open the door, and then relocates.
    2.  A humanoid robot enters the living room, moves to a table, picks up a fruit basket, and waits.
    3.  ROSClaw transmits environment perception results from a VLM to the arm agent, including fruit quantity, categories, and positions. It then invokes the DINO-X API from the online resource pool to provide grasping candidate points to the fixed robotic arm.
    4.  The fixed robotic arm, in a separate group, picks up a user-specified kiwi and places it into the basket, completing an inter-group collaborative interaction.
    5.  The humanoid robot then follows a navigation trajectory provided by ROSClaw to transport the fruit basket from the living room table to the kitchen sink.
*   **Results:** The ROSClaw framework successfully provided a unified solution for heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration. It effectively monitored user requirements, analyzed robot access regions, assigned tasks, facilitated inter-agent communication (e.g., VLM perception to arm agent), and invoked specific APIs for fine manipulation. The system demonstrated robust physical execution of the long-horizon, multi-step collaborative task across the diverse robotic platforms within a shared environment.

**B. Validation of e-URDF and the Data Collection and State Accumulation Mechanism:**
Two additional scenarios were designed to evaluate the physical safeguarding and data collection mechanisms.
*   **Fixed Robotic Arm Operation Group:**
    *   **Task Description:** A user interacted with ROSClaw and an arm agent. The user queried about fruits on a table. ROSClaw activated a RealSense camera and leveraged a VLM API to perform tabletop environment perception, responding with a structured list including color, category, and 3D positions of fruits. Based on subsequent user requests, the system decomposed the task into executable steps, logging execution trajectories and perception results for remote monitoring.
    *   **Results:** This scenario validated the real-time interaction capabilities, the integration of VLM for environmental perception, and the system’s ability to generate structured responses. Crucially, it demonstrated the data collection and state accumulation mechanism, which continuously recorded agent states and environmental perception data during execution.
*   **Gimbal-Based Multi-Agent Orchestration Group:**
    *   **Task Description:** A user requested "Gimbals dance." ROSClaw instantiated a Gimbal agent, invoked a music generation model, and launched Isaac Lab. An MCP service was created to interface with physical gimbal units and registered in the online resource pool. The system then loaded URDF models of the gimbals in Isaac Lab to continuously verify the physical safety of automatically generated choreography through simulation. Concurrently, ROSClaw connected to seven physical gimbal units via their interfaces from the online resource pool and executed the orchestrated dance motions in the real world.
    *   **Results:** This experiment effectively validated the e-URDF-based physical safeguarding mechanism by demonstrating pre-execution simulation and collision detection, ensuring the physical feasibility and safety of the generated motions. The framework reduced the time required to generate coordinated multi-gimbal dance behaviors to approximately three minutes, with minimal human intervention beyond initial instruction. This demonstrated efficiency in autonomous task generation and validation.

Overall, the findings indicate that ROSClaw effectively facilitates robust collaboration among heterogeneous agents, reduces development time by automating validation and program synthesis, and enables continuous learning through its integrated data collection and state accumulation capabilities.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The ROSClaw framework presents several significant contributions and holds substantial potential impact on the field of embodied artificial intelligence and robotics:

**Significance:**

*   **Bridging the Semantic-Physical Gap:** ROSClaw provides a cohesive framework that effectively connects the high-level semantic reasoning capabilities of large models with the low-level physical control requirements of real-world robots. This integration is crucial for robust task execution in complex, dynamic environments.
*   **Unified Control for Heterogeneous Systems:** By introducing the Online Tool Pool and a three-layer architecture, the framework offers a unified approach to abstract and coordinate diverse robotic platforms from different manufacturers, overcoming the challenges posed by fragmented SDKs and non-standardized interfaces.
*   **Enhanced Robustness and Safety:** The e-URDF-based physical safeguarding mechanism, coupled with digital twin simulation (Isaac Lab), is a key innovation. It ensures that physically infeasible or unsafe commands generated by large models are filtered out before execution, significantly improving the reliability and safety of robotic operations, especially for long-horizon tasks where error accumulation is a concern.
*   **Enabling Continual Learning from Physical Interaction:** The integrated data collection and state accumulation mechanism, leading to a Local Resource Pool, is fundamental for autonomous learning. It converts real-world physical interactions and execution trajectories into reusable knowledge, reducing reliance on costly manual annotation and demonstration and paving the way for self-improving robotic systems.
*   **Reduced Development Time and Cost:** The framework's ability to support hardware-level validation, automated SDK-level program synthesis, and rapid cross-platform skill transfer minimizes the need for extensive, robot-specific development workflows. This efficiency can significantly accelerate the deployment and adaptation of robotic systems.

**Potential Impact:**

*   **Acceleration of Embodied AI Research and Deployment:** By offering a more integrated, safe, and efficient framework, ROSClaw could accelerate the development and deployment of intelligent robots capable of performing complex tasks in human environments, such as smart homes, healthcare, and industrial automation.
*   **Advancement in Multi-Robot Collaboration:** The framework's capabilities in orchestrating heterogeneous multi-agent systems with robust inter-agent coordination and dynamic task assignment could lead to more sophisticated and flexible collaborative robotic applications.
*   **New Paradigm for Robot Learning:** The closed-loop learning mechanism, where physical experience directly informs and refines cognitive models, represents a shift from static, pre-programmed systems to continuously adapting and evolving embodied intelligence. This could foster the emergence of more generalizable and intelligent robotic behaviors.
*   **Accessibility and Scalability of Robotic Solutions:** By abstracting hardware complexity and facilitating skill transfer, ROSClaw could make advanced robotic capabilities more accessible to a wider range of developers and organizations, potentially scaling the adoption of intelligent robotic systems across various industries.
*   **Improved Human-Robot Interaction:** The ability to translate natural language instructions into safe and physically grounded multi-robot actions can enhance the intuitiveness and effectiveness of human-robot interaction.

While the current evaluations focus on structured or semi-dynamic environments, the framework lays a foundational groundwork for addressing more complex uncertainties and establishing truly closed-loop learning systems in future embodied AI research.
overview:
## Bridging the Semantic-Physical Gap in Embodied AI

The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) into robotics has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of "embodied agents." These agents can now interpret natural language commands for navigation and manipulation tasks. However, a fundamental challenge remains: the "semantic-physical gap." While LLMs are excellent at abstract reasoning, they often lack a grounded understanding of physical constraints, control dynamics, and hardware-specific limitations. Most current frameworks operate in disjointed stages—data collection, training, and deployment—which can lead to inconsistencies when a high-level plan meets low-level execution.

![The ROSClaw Architecture](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04664v1/The_ROSClaw_Architecture.png)
*Figure 1: The ROSClaw Architecture, illustrating the hierarchical flow from high-level cognitive reasoning to low-level physical execution through a coordination automation layer.*

ROSClaw addresses these issues by proposing a hierarchical semantic-physical framework designed for heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration. Unlike systems that rely on centralized schedulers with fragmented APIs, ROSClaw establishes a unified pipeline that integrates policy learning and task execution. This approach ensures that the "brain" (the cognitive model) is continuously informed by the "body" (the physical sensors and actuators), allowing for more robust performance in long-horizon, collaborative tasks.

## The Challenges of Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Collaboration

Modern robotic environments often require the coordination of diverse machines—for example, a humanoid robot with mobility and a robotic arm with precision. Coordinating these heterogeneous agents presents several hurdles:

1.  **Hardware Heterogeneity:** Different robots use different operating systems, drivers, and Software Development Kits (SDKs). A unified command for "moving an object" must be translated into specific, low-level joint torque or velocity commands for each unique hardware platform.
2.  **Semantic-Physical Mismatch:** A high-level plan might be linguistically sound but physically impossible. An LLM might command a small robot to lift a heavy weight, or suggest a path that results in a collision, because it lacks access to the robot's specific physical parameters.
3.  **Static Pipelines:** Many existing systems are open-loop. They execute a plan but do not effectively "learn" from the interaction to refine future reasoning.

ROSClaw tackles these challenges by introducing a three-layer architecture that separates high-frequency physical control from low-frequency semantic planning while maintaining a closed-loop feedback mechanism.

## The Three-Layer Architecture

The ROSClaw framework is organized into three distinct but interconnected layers: the Cognitive Layer, the Coordination Automation Layer, and the Physical World.

### The Cognitive Layer
At the top of the hierarchy is the Cognitive Layer, which acts as the decision engine. It utilizes LLMs and VLMs to perform task decomposition and long-horizon reasoning. When a user provides a natural language instruction, such as "prepare a snack in the kitchen," the Cognitive Layer breaks this down into an initial abstract task plan, denoted as $G_{LLM}$. This layer operates at a low frequency, focusing on "what" needs to be done rather than "how" to move every individual joint. It receives status feedback and environmental perception from the lower layers, allowing it to adjust the plan if an execution step fails.

### The Coordination Automation Layer (OpenClaw)
This intermediary layer, referred to as the OpenClaw system, is the core of the framework's flexibility. It features two primary components:
*   **Online Tool Pool:** This functions as a "digital dictionary." it aggregates various robot SDKs and Model Context Protocols (MCPs). It translates the abstract semantics from the Cognitive Layer into concrete software calls.
*   **e-URDF-based Physical Guard:** Before a command is sent to the physical robot, it must pass through this "physical firewall." Using an extended Unified Robot Description Format (e-URDF), the system validates whether a command is physically feasible.

### The ROSClaw Physical World
The bottom layer interfaces directly with the hardware via the Robot Operating System (ROS) and platform-specific drivers. It manages high-frequency control and data collection. This layer also populates the "Local Resource Pool," where real-time physical states, sensor data, and successful execution trajectories are stored. This data is essential for the framework's ability to "evolve" through continuous learning.

## Enforcing Physical Safety with e-URDF

One of the most critical contributions of ROSClaw is the use of e-URDF (extended URDF) to safeguard physical execution. Standard URDF files typically describe the kinematics and visual geometry of a robot. The e-URDF extends this by incorporating dynamic parameters such as payload limits, precision metrics, and control constraints.

![e-URDF Physical Guard Mechanism](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04664v1/e-URDF.png)
*Figure 2: The e-URDF Physical Guard mechanism, which validates tasks through forward dynamics simulation and collision detection before real-world execution.*

When the Cognitive Layer proposes a subtask, the Coordination Automation Layer uses the e-URDF representation to run a simulation in a digital twin environment (specifically Isaac Lab). The system checks a validation condition:

$$C_i^{e-URDF} \geq C_j^{req} \land s_j \in S_i$$

In this equation, $C_i^{e-URDF}$ represents the physical capabilities of robot $i$ (such as its reach or payload), while $C_j^{req}$ represents the requirements of subtask $j$. The term $s_j \in S_i$ ensures that the spatial location of the task $s_j$ falls within the robot's accessible workspace $S_i$. If the simulation detects a collision or a violation of physical limits, the command is rejected, and the Cognitive Layer is re-prompted to generate a safer alternative. This "forward dynamics simulation" prevents the error accumulation often seen in long-horizon tasks.

## Tool-Driven Execution and Program Synthesis

ROSClaw simplifies the development process for heterogeneous systems through its Tool-Driven Execution model. In traditional robotics, a developer must manually write code for every new task on every different robot. ROSClaw automates this by treating robot capabilities as "tools" in an Online Tool Pool.

When the Cognitive Layer identifies a need—for example, "detect objects on the table"—it searches the tool pool for the appropriate API, such as a DINO-X vision model or a RealSense camera driver. The framework can then synthesize SDK-level control programs on the fly. This "train once, deploy everywhere" paradigm allows for the rapid transfer of skills between different hardware platforms without requiring extensive manual tuning.

## Closing the Loop: Data Collection and the Local Resource Pool

A significant limitation of current embodied AI is the difficulty of obtaining high-quality, physically grounded data. ROSClaw addresses this through a "data flywheel" mechanism. During every task execution, the system continuously records:
*   **Robot Physical States:** Joint angles, velocities, and torques.
*   **Multimodal Observations:** RGB-D images and sensor readings.
*   **Execution Trajectories:** The exact path the robot took to successfully complete a subtask.

![Data Collection and State Accumulation](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04664v1/urdf.png)
*Figure 3: The data collection mechanism (Local Resource Pool), recording high-frequency states and environment perceptions to facilitate continual policy refinement.*

These records are standardized and stored in a Local Resource Pool. This pool forms a repository of reusable "skills." If the agent encounters a similar task in the future, it can retrieve these successful trajectories rather than reasoning from scratch. Furthermore, this data can be uploaded to a cloud-based model for large-scale fine-tuning, creating a closed-loop process where physical interaction directly improves the cognitive reasoning model.

## Experimental Validation in Complex Environments

To demonstrate the effectiveness of ROSClaw, the researchers conducted experiments in a $60\text{ m}^2$ smart home environment. The setup involved three heterogeneous robots from different manufacturers: a mobile robotic arm (Realman), a humanoid robot (Unitree G1), and a fixed robotic arm (Universal Robots UR5).

![Real-world Experimental Setup](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04664v1/realworld.png)
*Figure 4: The experimental smart home environment, showing the workspace partitioning for the mobile arm, humanoid, and fixed robotic arm.*

The environment was partitioned into different operating regions: $S_1$ for mobile area, $S_2$ for table operations, and $S_3$ for general pick-and-place regions. The task required complex collaboration:
1.  The mobile arm (Realman) approached a door, used its manipulator to open it, and moved aside.
2.  The humanoid robot (G1) entered the room and approached the table.
3.  The fixed arm (UR5) received instructions from a VLM to identify and pick a specific fruit (a kiwi) and place it into a basket held by the humanoid.
4.  The humanoid then navigated to the kitchen to deliver the fruit.

![Collaborative Task Execution](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04664v1/ROSClaw-Realman-UR5-G1.png)
*Figure 5: The sequence of collaborative execution between the Realman, UR5, and G1 agents, coordinated by ROSClaw to complete a multi-step delivery task.*

Throughout this process, ROSClaw managed the inter-agent communication and task assignment. It used the Online Tool Pool to invoke DINO-X for fruit detection and Isaac Lab to verify the UR5's grasping trajectory. The success of this long-horizon task across three different hardware platforms demonstrated the framework's ability to unify fragmented SDKs and maintain semantic consistency.

## Significance and Future Potential

ROSClaw provides a roadmap for more scalable and safe embodied intelligence. By decoupling high-level reasoning from high-frequency control while enforcing physical constraints through e-URDF, it bridges the gap between language and action. The integration of a data collection flywheel ensures that robots do not just perform tasks but actually learn from their physical environment over time.

This framework significantly reduces the barrier to entry for multi-robot collaboration. Instead of spending months writing custom drivers and scheduling logic, developers can leverage the Online Tool Pool and the hierarchical architecture to deploy complex behaviors across heterogeneous fleets. As robotic systems move out of controlled laboratories and into dynamic human environments, the safety, robustness, and learning capabilities provided by frameworks like ROSClaw will be essential for reliable operation.

keyInsights: Effective integration of high-level semantic reasoning with low-level physical control is achieved through asynchronous decoupling across a hierarchical architecture, maintaining stability and coherence.

A unified abstraction layer, the Online Tool Pool, successfully bridges diverse hardware SDKs and APIs, enabling cross-platform generalization and automated program synthesis from abstract instructions.

Proactive physical safeguarding via e-URDF and digital twin simulation is critical for ensuring the safety and feasibility of AI-generated actions, preventing physically impossible or unsafe commands from reaching real robots.
originalProblem: Existing embodied intelligence systems face a semantic-physical gap, struggling to connect high-level reasoning with explicit physical dynamics and control constraints, often leading to infeasible or unsafe plans.
Hardware heterogeneity, with fragmented SDKs and non-standardized interfaces across diverse robotic platforms, impedes unified control and efficient coordination.

Current frameworks are often static, open-loop pipelines, limiting continuous learning, adaptation from physical interactions, and effective generalization across dynamic environments for multi-agent systems.
results: The framework successfully orchestrated a complex, long-horizon collaborative task involving a mobile robotic arm, a humanoid robot, and a fixed robotic arm from different manufacturers in a smart home environment.

The e-URDF-based physical safeguarding mechanism validated automatically generated multi-gimbal dance choreographies through pre-execution simulation and collision detection, reducing generation time to approximately three minutes.

The system continuously recorded robot states and environmental perception data, enabling real-time interaction, structured environmental understanding via VLM, and the accumulation of reusable skill representations for continuous learning.
solution: A three-layer semantic-physical architecture (Cognitive, Coordination Automation via OpenClaw, and ROSClaw Physical World) unifies high-level reasoning, abstract task logic, and high-frequency robot control for heterogeneous agents.

An e-URDF-based physical interception and scheduling mechanism (a "physical firewall") uses digital twin simulation to validate the physical feasibility and safety of generated commands before execution.

A resource-driven closed-loop process continuously collects real-time robot states, multimodal observations, and execution trajectories into a Local Resource Pool, enabling self-organizing learning and continual policy refinement.
summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Research Institute for Intelligent Autonomous Systems developed ROSClaw, a hierarchical semantic-physical framework that integrates high-level AI reasoning with low-level robot control for heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration. The framework uses a vision-language model controller, incorporates physical constraints via e-URDF, and features a closed-loop data collection mechanism, enabling robust task execution and safe motion generation across diverse robotic platforms.

title: ROSClaw: A Hierarchical Semantic-Physical Framework for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Collaboration
